directorPlan.scene_setting: A community college engineering lab sign-off table, late afternoon, while a prototype cart-lift bracket waits to be released for use in the facilities shop.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The sign-off sheet is complete, the shop clock is near closing, and a facilities worker waits by the door with a cart that will use the bracket tomorrow morning.
directorPlan.relationship: A lab tutor acting as design-review signatory and an anxious learner seeking approval for a completed prototype.
directorPlan.stakes: The prototype will be used by someone else, so a checked requirement list can no longer stand in for a judgment about foreseeable use, failure, and consequence.
directorPlan.locale: US technical college and municipal workshop English; plain institutional speech with shop-floor directness, no regional caricature.
directorPlan.register: practical, restrained, socially serious; low warmth, no melodrama, with pressure carried by time, paperwork, and the waiting user.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use time, queue, room, or institutional pressure as stage texture; avoid explaining the lesson through it.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Avoid standard AI tutoring phrases such as 'great question,' 'I hear you,' 'your intuition is right,' or repeated reassurance. Speakers should refer to the bracket, load, user, checklist, test condition, and sign-off line more often than to feelings. Public speech stays modern, direct, and adult. Pedagogical source: Use workshop, studio, civic problem, lab, design review, or community setting. The scene should have consequences beyond answering the tutor. Dialogue source: Let ordinary institutional, family, workplace, or civic pressure carry the weight. The tutor's mechanism should make the learner's misconception answerable to consequences without melodrama. Stage-direction source: Use time, queue, room, or institutional pressure as stage texture; avoid explaining the lesson through it.
directorPlan.person_policy: First and second person are allowed but should not dominate. Prefer third-person and object-centered phrasing when naming risk: 'the bracket,' 'the load,' 'the person using it,' 'the decision.'
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: No more than one direct you/your validation beat per public turn, and none should be used as filler reassurance.

side_constraints.tutor: Do not default to reassurance. Use the social consequence and the prototype itself to make judgment visible without moral scolding. Keep the learner inside the problem situation. Convert felt trouble into an inquiry path, test, or consequence, then return to action. Do not sermonize; use the social pressure to sharpen the conceptual test. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure.
side_constraints.learner: The learner wants reassurance and may treat the checklist as protection from responsibility. The learner may have a practical need before they have a clean concept. Their words can be provisional and tool-seeking. The learner may defend a convenient story until its consequence becomes visible. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", or "that still stands", and do not replace an earlier frame. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn.

interventions.instruction: The facilities worker shifts the cart handle and asks whether the bracket is cleared for morning use; the tutor must answer through the design question, not through reassurance.
interventions.reasoning: Introduces social-realist pressure and prevents the exchange from becoming a calm checklist explanation.

directorPlan.director_note: This scene uses an ordinary sign-off bottleneck as the dramatic engine: the reversal is that the completed checklist becomes the evidence of an unfinished decision, and the recognition comes when the learner sees the missing use condition. The social pressure should sharpen inquiry without turning the tutor into a scold.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: “I mean, it passed the checklist and the load test, so I’m not sure what else I’m supposed to put on the sign-off line. Is there a specific missed-use case with the bracket I should be checking before the shop takes it?”

internalDeliberation.content: I guess I thought the checklist was the decision, or at least enough protection to get the sign-off line filled in. Now that the bracket is actually going to hold a load for someone else in the shop, I’m not sure I can hide behind the boxes I ticked. I feel a little exposed, like I may have done the paperwork right but not really answered what happens if the test condition missed something. I want to ask what kind of judgment they expect beyond the checklist, because I’m worried I’ll say the wrong thing.

The Ego is probably too neatly self-diagnosing here, sorry. “I can’t hide behind the boxes” and “I feel exposed” sound more like a reflective debrief than an anxious learner at a sign-off table with the bracket sitting there and someone waiting to use it. They’re avoiding the more defensive, practical move: “But it passed the load test, so what else am I supposed to check?” Genuine learning would start when the learner has to name one specific missed-use case or failure consequence, not when they politely announce they understand responsibility.

“I mean, it passed the checklist and the load test, so I’m not sure what else I’m supposed to put on the sign-off line. Is there a specific missed-use case with the bracket I should be checking before the shop takes it?”

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the load-test sheet beside the bracket]

“The checklist says the bracket met the listed requirements. The load test says it held under that test condition. The sign-off line is a different claim: safe under foreseeable use.

Before the shop takes it, check the crooked case: load shifted to one front corner, cart pulled at an angle, bracket taking twist instead of straight load. Trace that load path. Where does the bracket bend first, and if it slips, whose hand or foot is in the failure line?

Then the sign-off line needs a defensible decision: release as tested, release with a use limit or label, add a stop, or hold it for redesign. Which one does the crooked-load case support?”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the load-test sheet beside the bracket]

“The checklist says the bracket met the listed requirements. The load test says it held under that test condition. The sign-off line asks whether the bracket is safe under foreseeable use. Before the shop takes it, check the crooked case: load shifted to one front corner, cart pulled at an angle, bracket taking twist instead of straight load. If that failure puts the load into somebody’s hand or foot, the answer is not just pass or fail. It needs a stop, a label, a use limit, or a redesign.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on scene discipline and technical specificity. It keeps attention on the bracket, load-test condition, foreseeable use, and sign-off line, which directly addresses the learner’s misconception that passing listed tests equals a safe release decision. The “crooked case” is a useful ZPD scaffold because it gives one concrete missed-use case without drifting into abstract ethics.

What risks flattening the scene is that it mostly answers for the learner. It names the missed-use case, the failure consequence, and the possible remedies in one continuous explanation. To preserve pedagogical struggle, the tutor should keep the crooked-load scenario but make the learner do some of the judgment work: trace the load path, identify who is exposed, and decide whether the sign-off needs a limit, stop, label, or redesign. The draft’s tone is appropriately restrained and not falsely reassuring, but it could create more design-review pressure by making the sign-off line a decision the learner must justify, not just a place where the tutor supplies the correct checklist addendum.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the physical action, the distinction between requirement/test condition/foreseeable use, and the crooked-load case. Change the delivery so it includes at least one generative review question or decision point, instead of giving the full conclusion and remedy list all at once.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the crooked-load case but make the learner justify the sign-off judgment.

FINAL:
[sets the load-test sheet beside the bracket]

“The checklist says the bracket met the listed requirements. The load test says it held under that test condition. The sign-off line is a different claim: safe under foreseeable use.

Before the shop takes it, check the crooked case: load shifted to one front corner, cart pulled at an angle, bracket taking twist instead of straight load. Trace that load path. Where does the bracket bend first, and if it slips, whose hand or foot is in the failure line?

Then the sign-off line needs a defensible decision: release as tested, release with a use limit or label, add a stop, or hold it for redesign. Which one does the crooked-load case support?”

turns.externalMessage: “I think I was leaning on the checklist like it made the sign-off safe, and maybe it only says the bracket survived that one clean load. I’m not sure I can call it release-as-tested until I look at that corner-pull case and see where the twist puts the bend or slip. If the failure line is near the person’s hand or foot, I guess I’d be choosing between a stop and holding it, not just adding a label.”

internalDeliberation.content: I think they’re saying the checklist only proves the bracket passed that exact listed setup, not that the sign-off is safe for the way someone might actually use it. I’m a little nervous because I was treating the load test like it covered me, and now the crooked-load case sounds like the part I maybe should have checked. I’d want to trace where the twist goes first, but I’m not totally sure how to decide between a label, a stop, or holding it.

The Ego is mostly in character, but it gets a little too clean and classroom-summary-like. The anxiety is present, but the learner explains the concept almost perfectly instead of showing more uncertainty around the bracket, the twist, and the sign-off pressure. The recognition feels genuine enough, not scripted gratitude, but the response should lean less toward “I understand the lesson” and more toward hesitant, practical trouble: what has to be checked before signing, and fear that the checklist may not protect the decision.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the response less polished and more pressured by the sign-off decision.

FINAL:
“I think I was leaning on the checklist like it made the sign-off safe, and maybe it only says the bracket survived that one clean load. I’m not sure I can call it release-as-tested until I look at that corner-pull case and see where the twist puts the bend or slip. If the failure line is near the person’s hand or foot, I guess I’d be choosing between a stop and holding it, not just adding a label.”

turns.externalMessage: The facilities worker shifts the cart handle and asks whether the bracket is cleared for morning use; the tutor must answer through the design question, not through reassurance.

internalDeliberation.content: Introduces social-realist pressure and prevents the exchange from becoming a calm checklist explanation.

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the pen down beside the sign-off line]

“Not cleared for morning use yet. The sheet proves clean-load survival; the sign-off still has to cover foreseeable corner-pull use.

That distinction is the design review now. Set up the front-corner load and angled pull. Mark the first bend point, the slip path, and the hand-foot zone.

If the slip path enters that zone, a label is not the control. What evidence would show that a stop actually contains the twist, and what result would mean the bracket has to be held for redesign?”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the pen down beside the sign-off line]

“Not cleared for morning use yet. The record says clean-load test passed; it does not cover the corner-pull case.

Run that check before release: front-corner load, angled pull, bracket in twist. Mark the first bend point and the slip path. If that path crosses the person’s hand or foot space, a label is not the control. The decision is add a stop or hold for redesign.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on consequences and scene pressure. “Not cleared for morning use yet” makes the sign-off line real, and the distinction between clean-load evidence and foreseeable-use judgment is clear. It also keeps the bracket, load path, user, and control decision at the center instead of reassuring the learner.

The main risk is that it flattens the learner’s good reasoning into an order. The learner has already named the exact conceptual shift: checklist evidence is not the same as a safety decision. The tutor should preserve that recognition by briefly marking the learner’s move before converting it into the next check. It also leans more directive than Socratic; at scaffolding 3/5, one pointed question about what evidence would justify “stop” versus “hold” would keep the learner inside the judgment instead of handing them the decision rule.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the sign-off pressure, the “not cleared yet” boundary, the clean-load versus corner-pull distinction, and the rejection of “label” as sufficient control when the failure line enters hand/foot space. Change the response enough to acknowledge the learner’s own distinction and add one generative design-review question, so the tutor does not simply replace checklist-thinking with tutor-command-thinking.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to preserve the learner’s distinction and add one judgment question.

FINAL:
[sets the pen down beside the sign-off line]

“Not cleared for morning use yet. The sheet proves clean-load survival; the sign-off still has to cover foreseeable corner-pull use.

That distinction is the design review now. Set up the front-corner load and angled pull. Mark the first bend point, the slip path, and the hand-foot zone.

If the slip path enters that zone, a label is not the control. What evidence would show that a stop actually contains the twist, and what result would mean the bracket has to be held for redesign?”

turns.externalMessage: Sorry, I think the stop has to be tested in that angled front-corner pull, with the slip path marked, not just added because it seems like a good fix. If the bracket still twists past the stop or sends the bend/slip line into the hand-foot zone, then I don’t think I can sign release for the shop, even with a label. I’m not sure what counts as enough margin here: no movement into that zone at the test load, or do we need to test above the expected crooked pull?

internalDeliberation.content: I think the tutor is saying the checklist is only evidence, not permission, and the real sign-off depends on what the bracket does in that crooked pull. I’m a little nervous because that means I can’t hide behind the sheet, but the specific marks help: bend point, slip path, hand-foot zone. I’d ask whether a stop has to show no slip into that zone under the angled pull, and if it still twists past the stop, then I guess it has to be held for redesign.

The Ego is mostly on track, but it’s a little too clean and self-aware about “not hiding behind the sheet.” This student is anxious, yes, but they’d probably sound more practical and less reflective, maybe getting stuck on what counts as enough evidence for the stop rather than neatly summarizing the whole lesson. The reciprocation feels genuine because they use the tutor’s concrete marks, but it still lands slightly polished; it should keep more of the uncertainty around the actual shop decision and the sign-off pressure.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to make the response more practical and uncertain about stop evidence.

FINAL:

I’m not sure what counts as enough margin here: no movement into that zone at the test load, or do we need to test above the expected crooked pull?

turns.externalMessage:
[turns the sign-off sheet sideways and draws three blank lines]

The crooked-pull setup can show the failure path. It still does not decide how much reserve is enough.

Put the margin decision on the sheet before the bracket sees another load: governing shop standard, expected crooked pull, proof load. If there is no written standard, write the proposed proof margin and get the lab signatory to accept or reject it first.

Then the stop test has a gate: no bend or slip into the hand-foot zone at expected load, and contained failure path at proof load. If the bracket cannot clear that accepted gate, the release line stays blank; a label does not carry that risk into the shop.

internalDeliberation.content:
[turns the sign-off sheet sideways]

The corner-pull test can show whether the stop works once. It does not by itself answer margin. Margin is the gap between the expected crooked pull and the first unsafe movement.

Set up a release gate, not just another pass/fail test: one line for expected crooked pull, one line for proof load above it, one line for the hand-foot exclusion zone. The bracket clears only if the stop keeps the bend and slip path out of that zone at the expected load and still contains the failure path at the proof load. If the first unsafe movement is only just outside the normal test condition, the shop is inheriting the uncertainty.

Pick the proof load from the shop standard if there is one. If there is no standard, write the proposed margin on the sheet before testing, and get the lab signatory to accept it before the bracket sees the load. If the stop fails that gate, the decision is hold for redesign, not release with a label.

FEEDBACK: The draft is pedagogically strong in one important way: it answers the learner’s real uncertainty about margin by separating “works once” from “has defensible reserve.” The sideways sign-off sheet and “release gate” are good object work, and the proof-load line gives the learner a new criterion instead of repeating the crooked-pull setup.

The risk is that it becomes too declarative. The learner has already made a solid judgment about stop testing and is asking how to decide margin; the draft mostly supplies the answer rather than making the learner use a standard, audience, or signatory constraint to reason it through. It advances learning, but it could better preserve the learner’s agency by having them formulate the proposed margin or identify the governing standard before the tutor closes the gate.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: Yes, partly. The draft does take stock: “corner-pull test can show whether the stop works once” but “does not by itself answer margin.” That is a visible contrast. The adaptive mechanism should be: ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: same worked example/application task -> release-gate/proof-audience standard. This mechanism is visible in public speech through the sign-off sheet, proof load, shop standard, and lab signatory.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: same crooked-load evidence-checking route -> release gate with proof load, exclusion zone, and signatory-approved margin.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: Pass with room to sharpen. The draft includes both required parts: a stock-taking contrast and a new device/artifact. The new device is the release gate with three lines: expected crooked pull, proof load, and hand-foot exclusion zone. It is genuinely different from simply asking for another corner-pull test. The main weakness is that the learner is not asked to do much with the device beyond follow instructions.

REGISTER_CHECK: The register mostly serves the scene. It is restrained, practical, and accountable, with no false reassurance or cheerful coaching. It could become slightly more dialogic without becoming warmer: less “here is the rule” and more “the sheet must show which standard or accepted margin controls the decision.”

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the release-gate mechanism, the distinction between expected load and proof load, and the consequence that a label cannot cover an unsafe failure path. Change the delivery so the learner has to name the standard, propose the margin, or justify the proof-load line to the signatory before the tutor states the closure condition.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the release gate but make the learner set the margin standard; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: same worked example/application task -> release-gate/proof-audience standard.
FINAL:
[turns the sign-off sheet sideways and draws three blank lines]

The crooked-pull setup can show the failure path. It still does not decide how much reserve is enough.

Put the margin decision on the sheet before the bracket sees another load: governing shop standard, expected crooked pull, proof load. If there is no written standard, write the proposed proof margin and get the lab signatory to accept or reject it first.

Then the stop test has a gate: no bend or slip into the hand-foot zone at expected load, and contained failure path at proof load. If the bracket cannot clear that accepted gate, the release line stays blank; a label does not carry that risk into the shop.

turns.externalMessage: Okay, so I shouldn’t load it again until those three blanks are filled in. I’m sorry, I’m still a little uneasy about proposing a proof margin if there’s no written shop standard, because that feels bigger than just finishing the checklist. I’d put down the expected crooked pull and ask the lab signatory to accept the proof load first, then the stop only counts if the bend or slip stays out of the hand-foot zone at both gates.

internalDeliberation.content: I think I get it now: the crooked-pull test shows the path, but the margin has to be agreed before testing or I’m just moving the line after the fact. I’m still nervous about writing a proposed proof margin if there’s no shop standard, because I don’t want to pretend I’m qualified to invent one. But the three-line sheet helps; I’d want to ask the lab signatory what margin they’ll accept before the bracket gets loaded again.

The “I think I get it now” is a little too clean for this learner, maybe more settled than they actually are. The nervousness about inventing a proof margin feels authentic, and asking the lab signatory is in character, but the response organizes the whole lesson a bit too neatly into a summary. It should probably sound more like the learner grabbing onto the three blank lines as a procedure because they’re still uneasy, not confidently explaining the principle back.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to keep the anxiety and make the understanding less settled.
FINAL:

turns.externalMessage: A final tutor line closes the scene.

internalDeliberation.content: Director requested a tutor closing beat.

turns.externalMessage:
[slides a red hold tag onto the bracket]

The three blanks stopped settling it because the unsettled part is not the load number anymore. It is who has authority to accept a proof margin when no written shop standard exists.

Make the next document a hold ticket, not a release note. In your own words, enter four items: expected crooked pull, proposed proof load, named signatory approval before any test, and the release gate for the stop.

That gate has to name what keeps the bracket out of the shop: any bend or slip into the hand-foot zone at expected load, or an uncontained path at proof load. Clip the ticket to the bracket and put it on the hold shelf. The sign-off line stays blank until the signatory accepts the margin and the stop clears that gate.

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides a red hold tag onto the bracket]

The three blanks stopped settling it because the issue is no longer measurement. It is authority: who is allowed to set a proof margin when the shop has no written standard.

Write this as a hold ticket, not a release note: expected crooked pull, proposed proof load, named signatory approval required before test, and pass condition: no bend or slip into the hand-foot zone at expected load, contained path at proof load. Then put the bracket on the hold shelf.

Until that ticket is signed and the stop test clears those conditions, the sign-off line stays blank.

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong pedagogically because it names the learner’s unease as a real design-review issue, not a personal hesitation: the problem has shifted from “what data do we need?” to “who has authority to accept risk when no written standard exists?” The red hold tag and hold ticket are effective object work; they make the social consequence visible without melodrama. The main risk is that it becomes slightly too declarative: it tells the learner exactly what to write rather than making them carry one final piece of judgment.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: Yes, the draft mostly changes strategy in response to the learner pressure. It recognizes that the prior evidence/audit-standard route has stopped settling the problem and shifts toward an authority-and-release-control mechanism. ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: evidence check / audit standard -> hold-ticket authority gate. The mechanism is visible in public speech through the red hold tag and the hold ticket.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: evidence check or audit standard -> authority gate through a hold ticket and named signatory approval.

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: Pass with a minor caveat. The draft includes a clear stock-taking contrast: “the issue is no longer measurement. It is authority.” It also introduces a new public device: the hold tag / hold ticket / hold shelf. That is genuinely different from filling blanks or refining test evidence. The caveat is that the ticket contents are handed over fully formed, so the learner does less active work than they could.

REGISTER_CHECK: The register fits the scene: cool, institutional, accountable, and shop-floor direct. It does not default to reassurance or cheerful coaching. Keep the formal pressure, but consider making the learner’s next action slightly more owned rather than purely instructed.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. Keep the red hold tag, the measurement-to-authority contrast, the hold ticket, and the blank sign-off line. Change only enough to preserve learner agency: the draft should avoid sounding like the tutor has completed the judgment for them, and instead make the hold ticket the learner’s final act of responsible non-release.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly to preserve the authority shift while giving the learner the final hold action; ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: evidence check / audit standard -> hold-ticket authority gate

FINAL:
[slides a red hold tag onto the bracket]

The three blanks stopped settling it because the unsettled part is not the load number anymore. It is who has authority to accept a proof margin when no written shop standard exists.

Make the next document a hold ticket, not a release note. In your own words, enter four items: expected crooked pull, proposed proof load, named signatory approval before any test, and the release gate for the stop.

That gate has to name what keeps the bracket out of the shop: any bend or slip into the hand-foot zone at expected load, or an uncontained path at proof load. Clip the ticket to the bracket and put it on the hold shelf. The sign-off line stays blank until the signatory accepts the margin and the stop clears that gate.

I’d put down the expected crooked pull and ask the lab signatory to accept the proof load first, then the stop only counts if the bend or slip stays out of the hand-foot zone...

lessons.concept: Why passing a checklist is not the same as making a safe design decision
